misconceptionSummary: Students apply the associative law incorrectly to division, changing the value.

steps.narration: The student moved the brackets as if division is associative. But forty-eight divided by the quantity four divided by two is twenty-four, not six. Moving brackets changes division results.

visuals.ruleText: Division is NOT associative — brackets must stay where they are written
visuals.subText: a ÷ (b ÷ c) = a × c ÷ b ≠ (a ÷ b) ÷ c

steps.narration: Division in a bracket means dividing by the whole bracketed expression. When that bracket contains a division, removing it is not straightforward — the inner operation flips to multiplication.
Always evaluate the bracket first. Four divided by two is two. Then forty-eight divided by two is twenty-four. Brackets before anything else — that is the rule.

visuals.label: Division is not associative — evaluate brackets first

steps.narration: Twenty-four. Brackets always go first, and division brackets cannot be moved freely — evaluate what is inside before dividing.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: treating 48 ÷ (4 ÷ 2) as (48 ÷ 4) ÷ 2 = 6. Division is not associative. Evaluate the bracket first: 4 ÷ 2 = 2, then 48 ÷ 2 = 24.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Brackets cannot be moved in a division expression. Evaluate what is inside the bracket first — only then divide by the result.
